turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has our opinion in case 16-8255, McCoy v. Louisiana.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Petitioner Robert McCoy was indicted with murders of his estranged wife's mother, stepfather, and son. The state sought the death penalty. McCoy pleaded not guilty. Throughout the proceedings he maintained he was out of State at the time of the killings and that corrupt police officers killed the victims when a drug deal went wrong. After McCoy parted ways with appointed counsel and briefly represented himself, his parents engaged Larry English to represent McCoy. English concluded that the evidence against McCoy was overwhelming and that absent the confession that McCoy was the killer, at the guilt stage a death sentence would be impossible to avoid at the penalty phase. McCoy was furious when told two weeks before trial was scheduled to begin that English would concede McCoy's commission of the triple murders and argue for mercy in view of McCoy's mental and emotional issues. McCoy told English not to make the confession and sought to terminate English's representation. But the trial court refused to believing this and told him that he had to choose what trial strategy to pursue. At the guilt phase of the trial over McCoy's repeated and vociferous objection, English told the jury the evidence was unambiguous, McCoy committed three murders. English further said he took the burden off the prosecutor on the issue whether McCoy was the killer. English argued that McCoy should be convicted only of second-degree murder, because his mental incapacity prevented him from forming the specific intent to commit first degree murder. But Louisiana does not permit introduction of evidence of diminished capacity absent the entry of a plea of not guilty by reason of insanity. The jury found McCoy guilty of three first-degree murders, and that the penalty phase returned three death verdicts. The Louisiana Supreme Court affirmed. The question presented, did English's concession of McCoy's guilt over McCoy's unambiguous objection violate McCoy's constitutional right. We hold that it did. The Sixth Amendment guarantees to every criminal defendant the assistance of counsel for his defense. Even when a criminal defendant is assisted by counsel, some decisions are reserved for the client, those decisions include whether to plead guilty, waive the right to a jury trial, testify in one's own defense and forgo an appeal. Autonomy to decide that the objective of the defense is to assert innocence belongs in this category. Counsel may reasonably believe a confession of guilt is best suited to avoiding the death penalty as English did in this case. But the client may not share that objective. He may wish to avoid the opprobrium that attains admitting the murders of family members or believing that life in prison is not worth living and defendant may prefer to risk death for any hope, however small, of exoneration. When a client so decides and expressly asserts that the objective of his defense is to maintain innocence his lawyer must abide by that objective and may not override it by conceding guilt. Denial of a defendant's Sixth Amendment-secured autonomy to choose the objective of his defense ranks as error of the kind our decisions have called structural; and such error is not subject to harmless-error review. An error maybe termed structural if the right at issue is not designed to protect the defendant from erroneous conviction but instead to protect some other interest. Here the interest shielded is a defendant's autonomy to make basic choices about his own defense. McCoy must therefore be accorded a new trial without any need first to show prejudice. Because the Louisiana Supreme Court ruled otherwise we reverse that court's judgment. Justice Alito has filed the dissenting opinion in which Justices Thomas and Gorsuch have joined.
